news_summary: Recent advances in materials science and robotics showcase breakthroughs in graphene physics, improved robot swarm navigation, innovative chemical conversions, and enhanced 3D photonic integration.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes avancées en science des matériaux et en robotique mettent en évidence des percées dans le domaine de la physique du graphène, l'amélioration de la navigation des essaims de robots, des conversions chimiques innovantes et une intégration photonique 3D optimisée.
news_summary_es: Los recientes avances en ciencia de los materiales y robótica ponen de manifiesto avances revolucionarios en la física del grafeno, mejoras en la navegación de enjambres de robots, conversiones químicas innovadoras y una mayor integración fotónica en 3D.

articles.title: Graphene just defied a fundamental law of physics
articles.summary: In a major breakthrough, scientists have observed electrons in graphene flowing like a nearly frictionless liquid, defying a core law of physics. This exotic quantum state not only reveals new fundamental behavior but could also unlock powerful future technologies.
articles.content:
For decades, physicists have been trying to answer a fundamental question: can electrons move like a perfectly smooth, frictionless fluid governed by a universal quantum value? Detecting this unusual behavior has proven extremely challenging. In real materials, tiny imperfections such as atomic defects and impurities tend to disrupt these delicate quantum effects, making them nearly impossible to observe.

Now, researchers at the Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Science (IISc), working with collaborators from the National Institute for Materials Science in Japan, have finally identified this elusive quantum fluid in graphene. This material consists of a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a flat sheet. Their findings, reported in Nature Physics, open a new path for studying quantum phenomena and position graphene as a powerful platform for exploring effects that were previously out of reach in laboratory settings.

"It is amazing that there is so much to do on just a single layer of graphene even after 20 years of discovery," says Arindam Ghosh, Professor at the Department of Physics, IISc, and one of the corresponding authors of the study.

Breaking a Fundamental Law of Physics

To uncover this behavior, the team created exceptionally clean graphene samples and carefully measured how they conduct both electricity and heat. What they found was unexpected. Instead of increasing together, the two properties moved in opposite directions. As electrical conductivity rose, thermal conductivity dropped, and vice versa.

This result directly contradicts the Wiedemann-Franz law, a well-established principle that states heat and electrical conduction in metals should be proportional. The researchers observed deviations from this law by more than 200 times at low temperatures, revealing a striking separation between how charge and heat move through the material.

A Universal Quantum Connection

Despite this unusual split, the behavior is not random. Both types of conduction appear to follow a universal constant that does not depend on the material itself. This constant is tied to the quantum of conductance, a fundamental quantity that describes how electrons move at the smallest scales.

The Dirac Fluid and Liquid-Like Electrons

This remarkable effect occurs at a special condition known as the "Dirac point," where graphene sits at a boundary between being a metal and an insulator. By adjusting the number of electrons, researchers can reach this precise state.

At this point, electrons stop behaving like individual particles. Instead, they move collectively, flowing like a liquid. This fluid-like motion resembles water but with far lower resistance to flow. "Since this water-like behaviour is found near the Dirac point, it is called a Dirac fluid -- an exotic state of matter which mimics the quark-gluon plasma, a soup of highly energetic subatomic particles observed in particle accelerators at CERN," says Aniket Majumdar, first author and PhD student at the Department of Physics. The team also measured how easily this fluid flows and found that its viscosity is extremely low, making it one of the closest realizations of a perfect fluid ever observed.

A New Window Into Extreme Physics

These results establish graphene as an accessible and cost-effective system for exploring ideas that are usually associated with extreme environments. Scientists can now investigate phenomena linked to high-energy physics and astrophysics, including black-hole thermodynamics and entanglement entropy scaling, within a laboratory setting.

Future Applications in Quantum Technology

Beyond its scientific importance, this discovery could have practical implications. The presence of a Dirac fluid in graphene may enable the development of highly sensitive quantum sensors. Such devices could amplify extremely weak electrical signals and detect faint magnetic fields, opening the door to new technologies in sensing and measurement.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a major scientific breakthrough where researchers observed electrons in graphene behaving as a nearly frictionless quantum fluid, defying a fundamental physics law. This discovery not only advances fundamental physics understanding but also opens pathways for new quantum technologies with broad scientific and technological implications.
articles.personality_title: Scientists observe electrons flowing like a liquid in graphene, breaking physics rules
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For many years, scientists have wondered if electrons could move smoothly like a liquid without friction. Normally, tiny imperfections in materials stop this from happening, making it very hard to see.

**What happened** – Researchers at the Indian Institute of Science and their partners in Japan studied graphene, a thin sheet of carbon atoms. They made very clean samples and measured how electricity and heat moved through them. They found that electricity and heat moved in opposite ways, breaking a well-known physics rule called the Wiedemann-Franz law.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that electrons in graphene can act like a special liquid called a Dirac fluid. At this state, electrons flow together smoothly with almost no resistance, similar to water but much more perfect. This finding helps scientists study quantum physics in a simple material and explore ideas usually seen only in extreme places like particle accelerators or black holes.

**What's next step** – Scientists will use graphene to learn more about these unusual quantum fluids. They also hope to create new quantum devices, like sensors that can detect tiny electrical signals or weak magnetic fields, which could be very useful in technology and research.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Researchers have found that electrons in graphene can flow like a nearly frictionless liquid, breaking a key physics law and opening new paths for science and technology.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques observent des électrons couler comme un liquide dans le graphène, défiant les lois de la physique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis longtemps, les scientifiques se demandent si les électrons peuvent se déplacer en douceur comme un liquide sans friction. Habituellement, les petites imperfections dans les matériaux empêchent cela, ce qui rend cette observation très difficile.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Institut Indien des Sciences et leurs partenaires au Japon ont étudié le graphène, une fine couche d’atomes de carbone. Ils ont fabriqué des échantillons très purs et mesuré comment l’électricité et la chaleur y circulent. Ils ont découvert que l’électricité et la chaleur se déplaçaient de manière opposée, ce qui enfreint une loi physique bien connue appelée loi de Wiedemann-Franz.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que les électrons dans le graphène peuvent se comporter comme un liquide spécial appelé fluide de Dirac. Dans cet état, les électrons coulent ensemble sans presque aucune résistance, un peu comme de l’eau mais en beaucoup plus parfait. Cela permet aux scientifiques d’étudier la physique quantique dans un matériau simple et d’explorer des idées habituellement observées dans des environnements extrêmes comme les accélérateurs de particules ou les trous noirs.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont utiliser le graphène pour mieux comprendre ces fluides quantiques rares. Ils espèrent aussi créer de nouveaux dispositifs quantiques, comme des capteurs capables de détecter des signaux électriques très faibles ou des champs magnétiques faibles, ce qui pourrait être très utile en technologie et en recherche.

**Une phrase clé** – Les chercheurs ont découvert que les électrons dans le graphène peuvent couler comme un liquide presque sans friction, défiant une loi physique importante et ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour la science et la technologie.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos observan electrones fluyendo como un líquido en grafeno, rompiendo reglas de la física
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante muchos años, los científicos se han preguntado si los electrones pueden moverse suavemente como un líquido sin fricción. Normalmente, las pequeñas imperfecciones en los materiales impiden esto, haciendo que sea difícil de observar.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del Instituto Indio de Ciencia y sus colaboradores en Japón estudiaron el grafeno, una capa delgada de átomos de carbono. Hicieron muestras muy limpias y midieron cómo se movían la electricidad y el calor. Descubrieron que la electricidad y el calor se movían en direcciones opuestas, rompiendo una ley física conocida llamada ley de Wiedemann-Franz.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo muestra que los electrones en el grafeno pueden comportarse como un líquido especial llamado fluido de Dirac. En este estado, los electrones fluyen juntos con casi ninguna resistencia, parecido al agua pero mucho más perfecto. Esto ayuda a los científicos a estudiar la física cuántica en un material simple y explorar ideas que normalmente solo se ven en lugares extremos como aceleradores de partículas o agujeros negros.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos usarán el grafeno para aprender más sobre estos fluidos cuánticos inusuales. También esperan crear nuevos dispositivos cuánticos, como sensores que puedan detectar señales eléctricas muy pequeñas o campos magnéticos débiles, lo que podría ser muy útil en tecnología e investigación.

**Una frase clave** – Los investigadores encontraron que los electrones en el grafeno pueden fluir como un líquido casi sin fricción, rompiendo una ley física clave y abriendo nuevos caminos para la ciencia y la tecnología.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a single, delicate hexagonal graphene lattice floating above a calm, flowing river of shimmering liquid light, where glowing, smooth streams of tiny electrons move together like a gentle, perfect fluid, contrasting glowing trails symbolizing heat and electricity flowing in opposite directions within the lattice.

articles.title: This simple change stops robot swarms from getting stuck
articles.summary: In crowded environments, more robots don’t always mean faster results—in fact, too many can bring everything to a standstill. Harvard researchers discovered a surprising fix: adding a bit of randomness to how robots move can actually prevent gridlock and boost efficiency. By allowing robots to “wiggle” slightly instead of marching in straight lines, they can slip past each other and keep tasks flowing smoothly.
articles.content:
Imagine a swarm of robots rushing to complete an urgent job, such as cleaning up an oil spill or assembling complex machinery. At first, adding more robots speeds things up. But after a certain point, the space becomes crowded, robots start interfering with one another, and overall progress slows.

This raises a simple but important question: in a limited area, how many robots can you deploy before efficiency starts to drop? Researchers at Harvard believe they have found a clear answer.

A Simple Idea That Boosts Efficiency

A new study from the lab of L. Mahadevan, the Lola England de Valpine Professor of Applied Mathematics, Organismic and Evolutionary Biology, and Physics, shows that adding a controlled amount of randomness to how robots move can reduce congestion and improve performance in crowded environments.

The work combines mathematical modeling, computer simulations, and real-world experiments. It demonstrates how basic local movement rules can lead to organized, efficient outcomes on a larger scale. The findings could influence how robotic fleets are designed and may even apply to human crowd management and traffic flow. The research was published in Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences and led by applied mathematics Ph.D. student Lucy Liu, with guidance from SEAS Senior Research Fellow Justin Werfel.

Why Randomness Helps Predict Complex Behavior

Studying dense crowds is difficult because individuals can take countless possible paths and interact in unpredictable ways, Liu explained. To simplify the problem, the researchers treated each robot as a basic unit with a small, adjustable amount of variation in its movement.

"This might be counterintuitive, because how could randomness make things easier to work with?" said Liu. "But in this case, when you have a lot of randomness, it becomes possible to take averages -- average distances, average times, average behaviors. This makes it a lot easier to make predictions."

Simulating Robot Swarms in Motion

To explore this idea, the team created computer simulations of robot groups, referred to as agents. Each agent started at a random location and was assigned a random destination. Once it reached its target, it immediately received a new one, mimicking continuous task assignment in real-world systems.

Each agent moved toward its goal with a tunable amount of variation, described as "noise." With no noise, agents moved in straight lines. With high noise, their paths became erratic and inefficient. However, this wandering also helped them navigate around one another.

Finding the "Goldilocks Zone" of Noise

The simulations revealed a clear pattern. When agents moved in perfectly straight paths, they quickly formed dense clusters and traffic jams that halted progress. When movement became too random, congestion disappeared but efficiency dropped due to excessive wandering.

Between these extremes, the researchers identified a sweet spot. In this range, agents occasionally bumped into one another and formed short-lived clusters, but still managed to slip past and keep moving. This balance allowed the system to maintain a steady flow.

From Simulations to Mathematical Models

Using these insights, the team developed formulas to estimate "goal attainment rate," or how many destinations are reached over time. These equations made it possible to determine the ideal combination of crowd density and movement randomness to maximize performance.

Testing the Theory With Real Robots

To confirm their findings, Liu collaborated with physicist Federico Toschi at Eindhoven University of Technology in the Netherlands. Together, they set up experiments with small wheeled robots in a lab equipped with an overhead camera.

Each robot carried a QR code so its position could be tracked and updated with new destinations. Although the physical robots moved more slowly and less precisely than the simulated agents, they displayed the same overall patterns.

Simple Rules, Complex Results

The experiments supported a key idea: highly complex coordination does not require advanced intelligence or centralized control. Instead, simple local rules can produce effective group behavior, at least within certain density limits.

"Understanding how active matter, whether it is a swarm of ants, a herd of animals, or a group of robots, become functional and execute tasks in crowded environments using the principles of self-organization, is relevant to many questions in behavioral ecology," Mahadevan said. "Our study suggests strategies that might well be much broader than the instantiation we have focused on."

Implications Beyond Robotics

Liu noted that she has long been interested in designing safer and more efficient crowded spaces. This research points toward a future where the movement of large groups, whether robots, vehicles, or people, could be predicted and optimized using mathematical tools.

The results suggest that introducing controlled variability into movement patterns may improve flow in many real-world systems, from factory floors to city streets.

Key Takeaways

Harvard SEAS researchers found that when large numbers of robots operate in the same space, introducing a controlled amount of randomness in their movement can significantly improve efficiency.

The study highlights how simple, local movement rules can produce surprisingly complex and well-coordinated group behavior without the need for central control.

The mathematical models developed in this work could help optimize the design of robot swarms and even improve how we manage crowded environments like cities, traffic systems, and public spaces.

Funding for the research came from the National Science Foundation Graduate Research Fellowship Program under Grant No. DGE 2140743, along with grants from the Simons Foundation and the Henri Seydoux Fund.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant research breakthrough by Harvard scientists demonstrating that adding controlled randomness to robot swarm movement prevents gridlock and improves efficiency. This finding has broad implications for robotics, crowd management, and traffic flow optimization, offering tangible benefits for various real-world applications. The study is well-detailed, includes experimental validation, and proposes mathematical models that could influence future designs and urban planning, representing a meaningful positive impact on technology and society.
articles.personality_title: Adding randomness helps robot swarms avoid traffic jams and work better
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – When many robots work together in a small space, they can get stuck and slow down instead of finishing tasks faster. This happens because too many robots crowd the area and block each other.

**What happened** – Researchers at Harvard found that if robots move with a little bit of randomness—like a gentle wiggle instead of a straight line—they avoid getting stuck. They tested this idea using computer simulations and real robots in a lab. The robots carried QR codes so their movements could be tracked as they moved toward changing goals.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows that simple changes in how robots move can stop traffic jams without needing complicated control systems. It also means that large groups of robots, cars, or even people might move more smoothly if small random movements are added. The research offers a new way to design robot swarms and could help improve crowded places like city streets or busy factories.

**What's next step** – The team developed math formulas to find the best balance between crowd size and randomness to keep robots working efficiently. Future work might apply these ideas to real-world robots, traffic management, or crowd control to make movement safer and faster.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Adding a small amount of randomness to robot movements helps avoid jams and makes groups of robots work better together.
articles.personality_title_fr: Introduire de l’aléatoire aide les essaims de robots à éviter les embouteillages et à mieux travailler
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Quand beaucoup de robots travaillent ensemble dans un espace réduit, ils peuvent se bloquer et ralentir au lieu d’aller plus vite. Cela arrive parce que trop de robots se gênent et se bloquent mutuellement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de Harvard ont découvert que si les robots bougent avec un peu d’aléatoire—comme un léger mouvement de zigzag au lieu d’une ligne droite—ils évitent de se bloquer. Ils ont testé cette idée avec des simulations sur ordinateur et des robots réels en laboratoire. Les robots portaient des codes QR pour suivre leurs déplacements vers des objectifs changeants.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que de simples changements dans les déplacements des robots peuvent empêcher les embouteillages sans systèmes de contrôle compliqués. Cela signifie aussi que de grands groupes de robots, voitures ou même de personnes pourraient mieux circuler avec un peu d’aléatoire. Cette recherche offre une nouvelle façon de concevoir les essaims de robots et pourrait aider à améliorer les endroits bondés comme les rues ou les usines.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe a créé des formules mathématiques pour trouver le bon équilibre entre la taille du groupe et l’aléatoire afin de garder des robots efficaces. À l’avenir, ces idées pourraient s’appliquer à des robots réels, à la gestion du trafic ou au contrôle des foules pour rendre les déplacements plus sûrs et plus rapides.

**En résumé** – Ajouter un peu d’aléatoire aux déplacements des robots évite les blocages et aide les groupes de robots à mieux fonctionner ensemble.
articles.personality_title_es: Agregar aleatoriedad ayuda a enjambres de robots a evitar atascos y trabajar mejor
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cuando muchos robots trabajan juntos en un espacio pequeño, pueden atascarse y ralentizarse en lugar de completar tareas más rápido. Esto ocurre porque demasiados robots se bloquean entre sí.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de Harvard descubrieron que si los robots se mueven con un poco de aleatoriedad—como un ligero movimiento de zigzag en lugar de una línea recta—evitan atascos. Probaron esta idea con simulaciones por computadora y robots reales en un laboratorio. Los robots tenían códigos QR para seguir sus movimientos hacia objetivos que cambiaban.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque muestra que simples cambios en el movimiento de los robots pueden evitar atascos sin sistemas complejos de control. También significa que grandes grupos de robots, autos o personas podrían moverse mejor con un poco de aleatoriedad. La investigación ofrece una nueva manera de diseñar enjambres de robots y podría ayudar a mejorar lugares concurridos como calles o fábricas.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo desarrolló fórmulas matemáticas para encontrar el equilibrio ideal entre la cantidad de robots y la aleatoriedad para mantener la eficiencia. En el futuro, estas ideas podrían aplicarse a robots reales, al control del tráfico o a la gestión de multitudes para hacer el movimiento más seguro y rápido.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Agregar un poco de aleatoriedad al movimiento de los robots evita atascos y ayuda a que trabajen mejor en grupo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a swarm of small, stylized robots with simple geometric shapes moving smoothly and fluidly through a confined space, their paths gently weaving around each other with subtle, varied curves symbolizing controlled randomness, against a soft, natural color palette of muted blues, greens, and earth tones, conveying harmony and efficient flow without congestion.

articles.title: Carbonyl swapping converts cyclic ketones to saturated heterocycles
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 15 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10508-5Carbonyl swapping converts cyclic ketones to saturated heterocycles
articles.content: Saturated heterocycles are privileged scaffolds in bioactive molecules.1 Despite the availability of numerous de novo routes to various heterocyclic compounds, accessing diverse heterocycles from a unified, readily available carbocycle would offer a strategic alternative for constructing challenging heterocyclic structures from unconventional precursors.2 Here we report a modular approach that transforms a single cyclic ketone into various saturated heterocycles through formal carbonyl replacement with heteroatoms, via a scarcely explored bis(aroylperoxy) ketal intermediate. Through electronically guided peroxy cleavage, this intermediate enables double C–C bond scission of cyclic ketones, generating alkyl dichlorides as versatile handles for modular N/O/S/Se/Te incorporation using simple nucleophiles. This method exhibits broad substrate scope and functional-group tolerance, enabling both accelerated target synthesis and late-stage diversification of bioactive molecules. Its utility is also extended through “ring construction–carbonyl replacement” and “ring functionalization–carbonyl replacement” strategies, whereby cyclic ketones prepared via well-established methods are converted into challenging-to-access heterocycles for which analogous methods remain underdeveloped. By combining C–H oxidation with carbonyl replacement, a proof-of-concept formal “CH 2 -to-heteroatom” conversion is further demonstrated.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant chemical synthesis breakthrough enabling the conversion of cyclic ketones into diverse saturated heterocycles, which are important scaffolds in bioactive molecules. This modular approach offers a broadly applicable, versatile method with potential to accelerate drug discovery and development, impacting health-related fields and chemical manufacturing. The detailed description and demonstrated broad substrate scope indicate substantial substance and real-world impact beyond a niche audience.
articles.personality_title: New chemical method turns simple rings into important drug components
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists often need special ring-shaped molecules called heterocycles to create medicines. These molecules contain atoms like nitrogen or oxygen inside the ring and are found in many drugs. Making different types of these rings can be hard and usually requires starting from scratch each time.

**What happened** – Researchers have discovered a new way to change common ring-shaped molecules called cyclic ketones into many kinds of saturated heterocycles. They do this by swapping out a part of the molecule called the carbonyl group with atoms like nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur, or others. They use a special intermediate chemical that breaks certain bonds and creates spots to add these new atoms easily.

**Impact** – This method is important because it works on many types of starting molecules and can handle different chemical groups. It makes it faster and easier to build complex rings needed for medicines. Instead of making each heterocycle from the beginning, scientists can now start from one common molecule and create many variations. This could speed up the development of new drugs and help improve chemical manufacturing.

**What's next step** – The researchers plan to explore more ways to use this technique to make even more types of heterocycles. They also want to combine it with other chemical reactions to create new molecules that were hard to make before. This could lead to discovering new medicines and materials.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new chemical process transforms common ring molecules into diverse important drug components, simplifying and speeding up drug development.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle méthode chimique transforme des cycles simples en composants clés de médicaments
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques ont souvent besoin de molécules en forme d’anneau appelées hétérocycles pour créer des médicaments. Ces molécules contiennent des atomes comme l’azote ou l’oxygène dans l’anneau et se retrouvent dans beaucoup de médicaments. Fabriquer différents types de ces anneaux est difficile et demande souvent de repartir de zéro à chaque fois.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont découvert une nouvelle façon de transformer des molécules cycliques appelées cétones cycliques en plusieurs types d’hétérocycles saturés. Ils remplacent une partie de la molécule appelée groupe carbonyle par des atomes comme l’azote, l’oxygène, le soufre ou d’autres. Ils utilisent un intermédiaire chimique spécial qui casse certaines liaisons et crée des points où ces nouveaux atomes peuvent être ajoutés facilement.

**Impact** – Cette méthode est importante car elle fonctionne sur de nombreux types de molécules de départ et supporte différents groupes chimiques. Elle rend la fabrication d’anneaux complexes, nécessaires aux médicaments, plus rapide et plus facile. Au lieu de fabriquer chaque hétérocycle depuis le début, les scientifiques peuvent partir d’une molécule commune et créer plusieurs variantes. Cela pourrait accélérer le développement de nouveaux médicaments et améliorer la production chimique.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d’explorer davantage cette technique pour créer encore plus de types d’hétérocycles. Ils veulent aussi la combiner avec d’autres réactions chimiques pour fabriquer de nouvelles molécules difficiles à obtenir auparavant. Cela pourrait conduire à la découverte de nouveaux médicaments et matériaux.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un nouveau procédé chimique transforme des molécules cycliques communes en composants importants pour les médicaments, simplifiant et accélérant leur développement.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método químico convierte anillos simples en componentes clave para medicamentos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos a menudo necesitan moléculas en forma de anillo llamadas heterociclos para crear medicamentos. Estas moléculas contienen átomos como nitrógeno u oxígeno dentro del anillo y se encuentran en muchos fármacos. Fabricar diferentes tipos de estos anillos es difícil y generalmente requiere empezar desde cero cada vez.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores descubrieron una nueva forma de convertir moléculas llamadas cetonas cíclicas en varios tipos de heterociclos saturados. Lo hacen cambiando una parte llamada grupo carbonilo por átomos como nitrógeno, oxígeno, azufre u otros. Usan un químico intermedio especial que rompe ciertos enlaces y crea lugares para añadir fácilmente estos nuevos átomos.

**Impacto** – Este método es importante porque funciona con muchos tipos de moléculas iniciales y soporta diferentes grupos químicos. Hace que construir anillos complejos necesarios para medicamentos sea más rápido y sencillo. En lugar de hacer cada heterociclo desde cero, los científicos pueden partir de una molécula común y crear muchas variantes. Esto podría acelerar el desarrollo de nuevos medicamentos y mejorar la fabricación química.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean usar esta técnica para hacer aún más tipos de heterociclos. También quieren combinarla con otras reacciones químicas para crear nuevas moléculas que antes eran difíciles de fabricar. Esto podría llevar al descubrimiento de nuevos medicamentos y materiales.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo proceso químico transforma moléculas cíclicas comunes en componentes importantes para medicamentos, simplificando y acelerando su desarrollo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an elegant, glowing circular stone sculpture symbolizing a cyclic ketone, surrounded by delicate, colorful threads weaving through and transforming parts of the circle into distinct, softly glowing rings of different materials representing saturated heterocycles, set against a simple, natural background of gentle earth tones.

articles.title: Monolithic 3D integration of tantalum pentoxide nonlinear photonics
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 15 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10379-wMonolithic three-dimensional (3D) integration of tantalum pentoxide on a lithium niobate substrate enables scalable, multifunctional photonic systems and the incorporation of nonlinear optics directly into existing and emerging photonics infrastructure.
articles.content:
Jung, H. et al. Tantala Kerr nonlinear integrated photonics. Optica 8, 811–817 (2021).

Desiatov, B., Shams-Ansari, A., Zhang, M., Wang, C. & Lončar, M. Ultra-low-loss integrated visible photonics using thin-film lithium niobate. Optica 6, 380–384 (2019).

Wang, C. et al. Ultrahigh-efficiency wavelength conversion in nanophotonic periodically poled lithium niobate waveguides. Optica 5, 1438–1441 (2018).

Spencer, D. T. et al. An optical-frequency synthesizer using integrated photonics. Nature 557, 81–85 (2018).

Pfeifle, J. et al. Coherent terabit communications with microresonator Kerr frequency combs. Nat. Photon. 8, 375–380 (2014).

Raval, M., Yaacobi, A. & Watts, M. R. Integrated visible light phased array system for autostereoscopic image projection. Opt. Lett. 43, 3678–3681 (2018).

Alexander, K. et al. A manufacturable platform for photonic quantum computing. Nature 641, 876–883 (2025).

Moss, D. J., Morandotti, R., Gaeta, A. L. & Lipson, M. New CMOS-compatible platforms based on silicon nitride and Hydex for nonlinear optics. Nat. Photon. 7, 597–607 (2013).

Pfeiffer, M. H. P. et al. Photonic Damascene process for integrated high-Q microresonator based nonlinear photonics. Optica 3, 20–25 (2016).

Luke, K., Dutt, A., Poitras, C. B. & Lipson, M. Overcoming Si 3 N 4 film stress limitations for high quality factor ring resonators. Opt. Express 21, 22829–22833 (2013).

Belt, M., Davenport, M. L., Bowers, J. E. & Blumenthal, D. J. Ultra-low-loss Ta 2 O 5 -core/SiO 2 -clad planar waveguides on Si substrates. Optica 4, 532–536 (2017).

articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough in monolithic 3D integration of tantalum pentoxide nonlinear photonics on lithium niobate substrates, enabling scalable, multifunctional photonic systems. This advancement has broad implications for photonics infrastructure, potentially impacting telecommunications, computing, and sensing technologies on a large scale. The article provides substantial context and references, indicating a meaningful and well-documented innovation.
articles.personality_title: New 3D photonics technology combines tantalum pentoxide and lithium niobate for advanced light systems
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Photonics is the science of using light to send and process information, similar to how electronics use electricity. Scientists are always trying to improve photonic devices so they can be smaller, faster, and able to do more things at once. Two important materials for these devices are lithium niobate and tantalum pentoxide, which help control light in different ways.

**What happened** – Researchers have successfully combined tantalum pentoxide and lithium niobate into a single, three-dimensional (3D) integrated device. This means they built a tiny structure where these two materials work together on the same chip. The process is called monolithic 3D integration, and it allows the device to handle complex light signals and nonlinear optical effects, which are important for advanced functions like converting light wavelengths and processing signals.

**Impact** – This combination is important because it creates a scalable and multifunctional photonic system. In simpler terms, it means the technology can be made in large amounts and used for many different purposes. It can improve existing photonics infrastructure, which is used in things like faster internet, better sensors, and more powerful computers that use light instead of electricity.

**What's next step** – The next steps will likely involve testing these integrated devices in real-world applications and finding ways to manufacture them on a large scale. This could lead to new products that use light more efficiently for communication and computing, making technology faster and more capable.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have developed a new way to combine two powerful materials in a tiny 3D chip, opening the door to better and more versatile light-based technologies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Nouvelle technologie photonique 3D combine le pentoxyde de tantale et le niobate de lithium pour des systèmes lumineux avancés
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La photonique est la science qui utilise la lumière pour transmettre et traiter l’information, un peu comme l’électronique utilise l’électricité. Les scientifiques cherchent toujours à améliorer les dispositifs photoniques pour qu’ils soient plus petits, plus rapides et capables de faire plusieurs choses en même temps. Deux matériaux importants pour ces dispositifs sont le niobate de lithium et le pentoxyde de tantale, qui aident à contrôler la lumière de différentes manières.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont réussi à combiner le pentoxyde de tantale et le niobate de lithium dans un seul dispositif intégré en trois dimensions (3D). Cela signifie qu’ils ont construit une structure minuscule où ces deux matériaux fonctionnent ensemble sur la même puce. Ce procédé, appelé intégration 3D monolithique, permet au dispositif de gérer des signaux lumineux complexes et des effets optiques non linéaires, importants pour des fonctions avancées comme la conversion de longueurs d’onde lumineuses et le traitement des signaux.

**Impact** – Cette combinaison est importante car elle crée un système photonique évolutif et multifonctionnel. En termes simples, cela signifie que la technologie peut être fabriquée en grande quantité et utilisée pour de nombreux usages différents. Elle peut améliorer les infrastructures photoniques existantes, utilisées dans des domaines comme Internet plus rapide, de meilleurs capteurs et des ordinateurs plus puissants utilisant la lumière au lieu de l’électricité.

**Prochaine étape** – Les prochaines étapes consisteront probablement à tester ces dispositifs intégrés dans des applications réelles et à trouver des moyens de les fabriquer à grande échelle. Cela pourrait conduire à de nouveaux produits utilisant la lumière plus efficacement pour la communication et l’informatique, rendant la technologie plus rapide et plus performante.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont développé une nouvelle façon de combiner deux matériaux puissants dans une puce 3D minuscule, ouvrant la voie à des technologies lumineuses meilleures et plus polyvalentes.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva tecnología fotónica 3D combina pentóxido de tantalio y niobato de litio para sistemas avanzados de luz
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La fotónica es la ciencia que usa la luz para enviar y procesar información, parecido a cómo la electrónica usa electricidad. Los científicos siempre buscan mejorar los dispositivos fotónicos para que sean más pequeños, rápidos y capaces de hacer varias cosas a la vez. Dos materiales importantes para estos dispositivos son el niobato de litio y el pentóxido de tantalio, que ayudan a controlar la luz de diferentes formas.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores lograron combinar pentóxido de tantalio y niobato de litio en un solo dispositivo integrado en tres dimensiones (3D). Esto significa que construyeron una estructura muy pequeña donde estos dos materiales trabajan juntos en el mismo chip. Este proceso, llamado integración 3D monolítica, permite que el dispositivo maneje señales de luz complejas y efectos ópticos no lineales, que son importantes para funciones avanzadas como convertir longitudes de onda de luz y procesar señales.

**Impacto** – Esta combinación es importante porque crea un sistema fotónico escalable y multifuncional. En palabras simples, esto significa que la tecnología puede fabricarse en grandes cantidades y usarse para muchas aplicaciones diferentes. Puede mejorar la infraestructura fotónica existente, que se usa en cosas como internet más rápido, mejores sensores y computadoras más potentes que usan luz en lugar de electricidad.

**Próximo paso** – Los siguientes pasos probablemente serán probar estos dispositivos integrados en aplicaciones reales y encontrar formas de fabricarlos a gran escala. Esto podría llevar a nuevos productos que usen la luz de manera más eficiente para comunicación y computación, haciendo la tecnología más rápida y capaz.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos han desarrollado una nueva forma de combinar dos materiales poderosos en un chip 3D pequeño, abriendo el camino a tecnologías basadas en luz mejores y más versátiles.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of an intricate, glowing network of layered waveguides and microresonators made of smooth, translucent materials symbolizing tantalum pentoxide and lithium niobate, interconnected by flowing streams of light pulses weaving through the layers like delicate ribbons, set against a soft, neutral background that evokes a serene laboratory environment focused on advanced photonic integration.
